3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Ganson, K. T., Iyer, P., Chu, J., Baker, F. C., Garber, A. K., Murray, S. B., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Gabriel, K. P. (2022). Sociodemographic correlates of contemporary screen time use among 9- and 10-year-old children. The Journal of Pediatrics, 240, 213-220.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Cross-sectional baseline data were analyzed from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (3.0 release; 2016-2018, ages 9-10 years)... Participants with missing data for sociodemographic or screen time variables were excluded (Table I; available at www.jpeds.com), leaving a total of 10 755 in the cohort.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Sociodemographic factors (sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parent education, BMI)
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - exposure is sociodemographic, outcome is screen time
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Sex (male or female), race/ethnicity (White, Latinx/Hispanic, Black, Asian, Native American, other), and country of birth (US or outside US) were based on parent report. Household income and highest parent education were based on parents' report.
3C_Exposure.notes: This paper examines sociodemographic predictors of screen time - screen time is the OUTCOME, not the exposure

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Screen time by modality (total, TV, YouTube, video games, social networking, texting, video chat)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Self-reported screen time was determined from the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey, based on a previously validated measure. Participants reported typical hours per day spent on 6 different screen modalities (viewing/streaming television shows or movies, watching/streaming videos [eg, YouTube], playing video games, texting, video chatting [eg, Skype, Facetime], and social networking [eg, Facebook, Instagram, Twitter]) separately for weekdays and weekend days.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple linear regression with propensity weights; effect modification by income tested
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parent education, BMI, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple linear regression analyses were conducted to estimate associations between sociodemographic factors (sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education, body mass index) and 6 contemporary forms of screen time (television, videos [eg, YouTube], video games, social networking, texting, and video chat), adjusting for site. We tested for effect modification by income in the association between race/ethnicity and screen time, as well as sex and screen time, and presented income-stratified results given evidence of significant effect modification (P < .05).

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table III: Male B=0.75 (0.63 to 0.89), p<0.001 for total screen time
Table III: Black B=1.58 (1.35 to 1.79), p<0.001 for total screen time
Table III: Asian B=-0.35 (-0.62 to -0.09), p=0.008 for total screen time
Table III: Less than $75,000 B=0.91 (0.77 to 1.05), p<0.001 for total screen time

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: High school education or less (vs college+)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table III: High school education or less B=0.28 (0.06 to 0.49), p=0.012 for total screen time

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: It is important to note that the effect sizes of some of the less-used screen time modalities were small.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Policies and guidelines for screen time use should consider the unique differences found in this study to inform individualized counseling and implementation efforts. The American Academy of Pediatrics Family Media Use Plan could be adapted for families with fewer resources.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion focuses on describing sociodemographic disparities in screen time use among 9-10 year olds. Key findings include higher screen time in Black children, boys, and lower-income families. It discusses structural factors (neighborhood safety, fewer recreational opportunities) that may explain disparities and calls for tailored policies rather than one-size-fits-all screen time guidelines. The paper appropriately notes small effect sizes for some modalities and acknowledges cross-sectional limitations.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Screen time is the outcome variable, not the exposure. This is a descriptive epidemiology paper on screen time correlates, not a screen time effects paper.
